---
title: "Title of Project (TBD)\\vspace{-2cm}"
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: "@*"
csl: ieee.csl
execute:
  echo: false
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \RedeclareSectionCommand[beforeskip=0.5em, runin=false, afterskip=0em]{section}
        \usepackage{fullpage}
        \usepackage{wrapfig}
number-sections: true
---
## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The Intern Health Study (IHS) examines the health outcomes of medical interns who experience high levels of stress, and evaluates reinforcement learning–based mobile health interventions aimed at improving mood, sleep, and physical activity. Although data are collected from three wearable devices (Garmin, Apple, and Fitbit), we focus on Fitbit data for this analysis.

This project focuses on latent physical activity, which we measure using step counts. A key challenge in the data is that hourly step counts are not recorded when participants are not wearing their devices. As a result, the RL model lacks a complete 24-hour reward signal and cannot directly assess whether physical activity has improved.

We explore four models, ARMA, no pooling, partial pooling, and global pooling, to estimate the over-dispersion parameter $k$ for each participant. Our research goal is to predict rewards in the presence of missing data, making this a prediction task. The primary performance metric is the masked subtotal error, which masks 20\% of observed hourly data and computes the prediction error. Additional metrics include the correlation between observed and predicted step counts at both the daily and hourly levels, as well as the log-likelihood.

[interface here:
selects RL = 0, 1, or 2
RL = 0 means]

# Introduction

## Background Information and Project Motivation

<!-- 
Intern Health Study (IHS) 2024 -> data on ~1000 new doctors

three domains (mood, sleep, and activity) but we are only focusing on activity

three wearable devies (garmin, apple, fitbit) -> disconnect in devices

at 3 pm, individuals have a 50% change of receiving a message (decided by an agent) 

goal: maximize step count which is the reward (I think?)
-->

For a new medical school graduate, the first year of residency is demanding and stressful. The yearly Intern Health Study (IHS) aims to study the effect of stress on mental health outcomes, enrolling over a thousand new interns each year. @internhealthstudy The study collects large amounts of data on interns, such as quarterly psychological surveys, DNA samples, daily mood scores, and sleep and physical activity data from wearable devices. In this project, we focus only on physical activity measured by step count, in the 2024 IHS.

In addition to collecting data, recent years are incorporating reinforcement learning based mobile health interventions. At 3:00 p.m. each day, an agent decides based on the participant's context whether to send a patient a message, with some learned probability. This randomized setup allows researchers to study whether these messages influence participants' behavior. In this context, the objective is to increase physical activity, so step count serves as the reward outcome of interest. To be precise, the "reward" for a given day is the number of steps a user takes in the 24 hours after the 3pm decision point in local time. These rewards are not needed at decision time for the RL models, but can be backfilled and trained on later, even months later potentially.

<!-- 
RoME is a robust contextual bandit method for health apps that decides when to send notifications and how to personalize interventions based on the user’s current situation. @RoME

Oralytics, a mHealth intervention system, uses an online RL algorithm to determine optimal times for delivering interventions to promote oral self-care behaviors. @Oralytics

Missing data may result in distortions that alter algorithm behavior. Robust strategies are needed to handle delayed and missing data because the algorithm needs to continue functioning despite these challenges and ensure reproducibility. @DigitalHealth
-->

Reinforcement learning and contextual bandit methods are becoming increasingly important in healthcare because they enable personalized interventions. In digital health, these methods can be used to decide when and how to deliver prompts that encourage healthier behaviors, such as increasing physical activity or improving medication adherence. Contextual bandits are especially useful because they incorporate patient characteristics when selecting an action. For example, RoME is a robust contextual bandit method for mobile health applications that determines when to send notifications and how to personalize interventions based on a user’s current situation. @RoME Similarly, Oralytics is a mobile health intervention system that uses an online reinforcement learning algorithm to determine optimal times to deliver interventions that promote oral self-care behaviors. @Oralytics

This project is motivated by the growing need to apply these methods in realistic settings where data is often incomplete. In our dataset, missingness occurs when users do not wear their devices which leads to gaps in the recorded activity data. Missing data can distort the information available to the algorithm and may ultimately affect its behavior and performance. As a result, robust strategies for handling delayed and missing data are essential so that the algorithm can continue to function reliably and produce reproducible results. @DigitalHealth

## Past Project Context

<!-- 
1. [Heart Rate Variability](https://ionides.github.io/531w25/final_project/project10/blinded.pdf)

LG-POMP model; Apple measurements only
Peer review comments - more diagnostic plots, like looking at likelihood anomalies
No description of time zone handling
-->

Most past projects centered on infectious disease or financial topics. There was one project that also looked at measurements from wearable devices @HeartRate. That project focused on the instantaneous effect of environmental noise on heart rate variability (HRV), while ours is primarily about predicting step count in the next 24 hours following a motivating message sent to study participants.

Both projects face the same issue of missing data. The HRV project pooled all participants together into a population-level daily summary, taking the median measurement of each day. While we initally designed Partially Observed Markov Process (POMP) models for individual participants, we also incorporated global and partial pooling to jointly learn measurement dispersion parameters across participants in a highly compute-intensive, high parameter "panel pomp". In our search, we didn't find any projects which focused on prediction of missing data, nor which implemented a panel pomp. Because of this, we focused less on model diagnostics and scientific questions answered by inference, instead performing model selection by predictive ability, using predictions on masked data.

## Research Question

When a participant does not wear the watch, no step count data are collected during that period. On days when the watch is worn for only a short time, or not at all, a reward (the number of steps taken in the 24 hours after 3pm) cannot be constructed, so that day provides no training information for the reinforcement learning-based intervention. The goal of this project is to build personalized POMP models for each doctor and to estimate the missing rewards.

# Exploratory Data Analysis


Participants wear devices from Garmin, Fitbit, and Apple, and some individuals may use more than one device. Since these devices differ in how they record and store data, as well as behavioral differences in device ownership, the data from multiple devices cannot be combined perfectly. While it would be worthwhile to explore joint measurement models for each data stream, due to time and compute constraints, we chose to limit ourselves to users of a single device.



For this project, we focused only on users who had Fitbit data for several reasons. Since not many users had Garmin devices, it would be difficult to train the model with such limited data. Of the approximately 1,000 doctors in the data, only 88 used Garmin. Apple HealthKit acts as a central repository for health and fitness data which combines information from both phones and watches. In other words, different apps are allowed to sync and aggregate data. For example, HealthKit can integrate with other wearables such as Garmin, so the step data may not even originate from an Apple Watch. We found it difficult to disentangle the biases associated with this device. 

The FitbitHourly.csv file contains data from 299 participants with Fitbit data. We limited our analysis to the period from July 1, 2024, to September 30, 2024, the first three months of the IHS 2024. Additionally, we filtered for participants with Fitbit step data in at least half of their hourly rows, which left us with 78 participants.

<!--
(PRUNE FitbitHourly.csv to only July 1 to September 30, readjust back to local time)

The FitbitHourly.csv dataset contains wearable data from July 1, 2024 to September 30, 2024 for 299 participants. We analyze the 78 participants who have Fitbit steps data for at least half of their hourly rows. 

Notes:
* sample statistics like mean steps, std, mean steps per hourly non-missing bucket, missingness, maybe box plot for missingness (min, max, quartiles).
-->

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: eda-libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import re
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: eda-read-data

# read in dataset
df = pd.read_csv("FitbitHourly.csv")

# prune for July 1 to September 30 and readjust back to local time
prune_df = df[(df["date_utc"] >= "2024-07-01") & (df["date_utc"] <= "2024-09-30")]

# participants with Fitbit steps data for at least half of their hourly rows -> need at least 1104 rows filled out
valid_ids = (
    prune_df.groupby('PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER')['fitbitStepsMissing']
      .sum()
      .loc[lambda x: x <= 1104]
      .index
)
prune_df = prune_df[prune_df['PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER'].isin(valid_ids)]
```

The chart below is a scatterplot of the mean and standard deviation of hourly steps for each user after filtering for hours with non-missing data. The means are quite a bit larger than you'd expect if these are governed by an iid Poisson model, which motivates the use of a latent stochastic process and a measurement model with room for dispersion.

```{python}
# | echo: false

# scatter plot of mean versus standard deviation of fitbit steps for non-missing buckets
# each dot represents one of the 78 participants
non_missing = prune_df.loc[prune_df["fitbitStepsMissing"] == 0].copy()
non_missing["fitbitSteps"] = pd.to_numeric(non_missing["fitbitSteps"], errors="coerce")
non_missing = non_missing.dropna(subset=["fitbitSteps"])

stats = non_missing.groupby("PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER")["fitbitSteps"].agg(
    ["mean", "median", "std", "min", "max", "count"]
).reset_index()

plt.scatter(stats["mean"], stats["std"])
plt.xlabel("Mean Fitbit Steps")
plt.ylabel("Standard Deviation of Fitbit Steps")
plt.title("Mean vs Variability in Steps (Non-Missing Hourly Buckets)")
plt.show()
```

Here is a boxplot of the percentage of hours that have missing step count data for each user.

```{python}
missing = prune_df[prune_df['fitbitStepsMissing'] == 1]
counts = missing.groupby('PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER').size()
percent_missing = (counts / 2208) * 100

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sns.boxplot(y=percent_missing)
plt.ylabel('Percent Missing (%)')
plt.title('Missing Data per Participant')
plt.show()
```

# Model Design

Our aim is to estimate the "24-hour-reward", the sum of the steps in the 24 hours following 3pm local time. We work at the hourly level, so each hour is treated as one bucket. Fitbit step counts may be observed data may be observed or missing in a given bucket, which motivates a partially observed Markov process (POMP) model:

We write the latent activity state as:
$$
x_{t+1} = \phi x_t + \sigma \varepsilon_t,
\qquad \varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1),
$$
where $x_t$ represents hour-to-hour deviations in activity not explained by systematic daily structure; $\phi$ controls temporal persistance and $\sigma$ controls the magnitude of unexplained hourly variation.

Conditional on the latent state, the latent step count in hour $t$ is:
$$
N_t | x_t \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_t),
\qquad \log \lambda_t = \alpha_{h(t)} + \delta_{w(t)} + x_t
$$
where
$$
\log \lambda_t = \bar{\ell} + \alpha_{h(t)} + \delta_{w(t)} + x_t
$$

Here, $h(t)$ is the local hour of day and $w(t)$ is the local day of the week. The $\alpha_{h(t)}$ term captures participant-specific hour-of-day patterns, and $\delta_{w(t)}$ captures participant-specific day-of-week effects, estimated empirically as follows:

$$
\ell_t = \log(\text{steps}_t + 1)
$$

For each hour $h \in \{0, \dots, 23\}$, hour-of-day effect is centeed by $\bar{\ell}_t$ as:
$$
\alpha_h 
= 
\mathbb{E} [\ell_t \mid h(t) = h] - \bar{\ell} 
- \frac{1}{24}
\sum \limits_{r = 0}^{23} \alpha_r
$$

The weekday effects are computed similarly.

The observed Fitbit step count is modeled as a noisy measurement of the latent hourly steps:
$$
Y_t | N_t \sim \text{NegBin}(N_t, k)
$$

We use the negative binomial parametrization with mean $N_t$ and variance 
$$
\mathrm{Var}(Y_t \mid N_t) = N_t + \frac{N_t^2}{k}.
$$

Under this parametrization, larger values of $k$ correspond to more $Poisson$-like variation. The plot above (mean vs variability in steps, what figure number?) suggested to us that 

Our quantity of interest is the latent 24-hour reward:
$$
R_d = \sum \limits_{t \in H(d)} N_t
$$

where $H(d)$ denotes the 24 hourly buckets following 3pm local time. Because the reward is defined using the latent counts $N_t$, the model can be used to reconstruct daily rewards even when hourly Fitbit observations are missing.

All 78 participants are jointly fit to personalized models in a "panel". We compare three variations of the model all sharing the AR(1) latent that differ in their measurement distribution, in how the observation-dispersion $k$ is handled: participant-specific $k_i$ with no pooling, a globally shared $k$, and a partially pooled $k_i$. We refer to these as ar1personal, ar1global, and ar1partial respectively. Among the parameters $(\phi, sigma, k)$, $k$ was the most natural candidate for pooling because it governs the measurement dynamics of the Fitbit, which all participants are using.

In the no-pooling model, each participant has their own $\phi_i$, $\sigma_i$, and $k_i$. Here, the panel main serves as a convenient interface to fit many participant-specific, independent time series, but there is no information sharing across individuals. By contrast, in the globally pooled model, all participants share a single common value of $k$ learned jointly across all participants. This treatments Fitbit dispersion as a common feature across users.

In partial pooling, each participant still has an individual dispersion parameter $k_i$, but they come from an enforced shared distribution. We set $k$ on a log scale as $\eta_i = \log k_i$, and assume $\eta_i \sim N(\mu_k, \tau_k^2)$. This enters the objective as a penalty:

$$
\sum \limits_{i = 1}^n
\ell_i(\phi_i, \sigma_i, e^{\eta_i})
-\frac{1}{2\tau_k^2}
\sum \limits_{i = 1}^n(\eta_i - \mu_k)^2
- n \log \tau_k
$$
(check the -n log tau)

So the values of $\log k_i$ are pushed towards the common mean $\mu_k$; when $\tau_k$ is small, the shrinkage is strong and the model approach the global pool regime, while large $\tau_k$ implies weak shrinkage and the model approaches the no-pooling regime. Here, the filtering aims to learn the $\mu_k, \tau_k$ that maximizes this joint objective.

Finally, as rewards do not need to be calculated at decision-time, we can use future information, information from events occurring after the decision point, to improve our estimates. This is a smoothing task. So in all, we run these three model variations each with a filter, and a smoother, and evaluate their performance.

# Individual Participant Demonstrations

outline 2:
1) Paragraph explaining what we're doing.

2) Show plot of reconstructed rewards for both high missingness and low missingness, one paragraph to discuss both.

3) Show plot of missingness vs residual, paragraph to discuss.

(stretch goal 4, show some diagnostics)

```{python}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| label: set-controls
# (set XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=HW8 in bash to render quarto on greatlakes)

# Cache controls
enable_shelve_cache = True
clear_shelve_cache = False
cache_version_token = "highmiss_cache_v1"
shelve_cache_directory = ".highmiss_cache"
shelve_cache_basename = "highmiss_4796"

# Participant and model controls
participant_id = "MDH-3531-4796"
analysis_start_utc_iso = "2024-07-01T00:00:00+00:00"
analysis_end_utc_iso = "2024-09-30T23:00:00+00:00"
participant_data_lookback_hours = 48
assumed_local_timezone = "America/New_York"
participant_covariate_keys = []  # Example: ["trail_steps_24h", "send_lag_0_3h"]
estimated_free_params = ["phi", "sigma", "k_fitbit"]
initial_phi = 0.75
initial_sigma = 0.30
initial_k_fitbit = 200.0

# Local MLE controls
local_mle_particles = 100
local_mle_mif_iterations = 6
local_mle_random_seed = 11
local_mle_cooling_fraction = 0.50
local_mle_rw_sd_scale = 0.02
local_mle_evaluation_particles = 100
local_mle_evaluation_pfilter_reps = 3

# Global MLE search controls
global_search_random_seed = 37
global_search_include_current_theta_as_start = True
global_search_jitter_scale = 0.10
global_search_box_shrink_factor_per_stage = 1.00

# Global MLE stage 1 controls
global_stage1_name = "Stage 1"
global_stage1_n_starts = 10
global_stage1_keep_top = 3
global_stage1_particles = 100
global_stage1_mif_iterations = 4
global_stage1_cooling_fraction = 0.50
global_stage1_rw_sd_scale = 0.02
global_stage1_evaluation_particles = 200
global_stage1_evaluation_pfilter_reps = 2
global_stage1_n_fresh_random_starts = 0
global_stage1_n_jittered_restarts = 0

# Global MLE stage 2 controls
global_stage2_name = "Stage 2"
global_stage2_n_starts = 4
global_stage2_keep_top = 2
global_stage2_particles = 160
global_stage2_mif_iterations = 8
global_stage2_cooling_fraction = 0.50
global_stage2_rw_sd_scale = 0.015
global_stage2_evaluation_particles = 320
global_stage2_evaluation_pfilter_reps = 3
global_stage2_n_fresh_random_starts = 1
global_stage2_n_jittered_restarts = 1

# Global start-box controls
global_start_box_phi_low = -0.95
global_start_box_phi_high = 0.95
global_start_box_sigma_low = 0.05
global_start_box_sigma_high = 2.00
global_start_box_k_fitbit_low = 1.0
global_start_box_k_fitbit_high = 50.0

# Reconstructed reward plot controls
reward_plot_fit_source = "global"  # "local" or "global"
reward_plot_fig_width = 18.0
reward_plot_fig_height = 9
reward_plot_title_fontsize = 25
reward_plot_axis_label_fontsize = 22
reward_plot_tick_fontsize = 20
reward_plot_legend_fontsize = 20

# Residual-vs-missingness plot controls
residual_plot_fit_source = "global"  # "local" or "global"
residual_plot_fig_width = 5
residual_plot_fig_height = 4
```

```{python}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Everything needed to run the analysis is defined in this qmd. The only data
# file used below is FitbitHourly.csv.
import copy
from functools import lru_cache
import hashlib
import json
from pathlib import Path
import shelve
from dataclasses import dataclass
from time import perf_counter

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import display
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax.scipy.special import gammaln
from pypomp import Pomp, ParTrans, RWSigma

# CHANGE PATH
CSV_PATH = Path("FitbitHourly.csv")
if not CSV_PATH.exists():
    raise FileNotFoundError("FitbitHourly.csv must be in the same folder as this qmd.")

CSV_CACHE_SIGNATURE = {
    "csv_size_bytes": int(CSV_PATH.stat().st_size),
    "csv_modified_ns": int(CSV_PATH.stat().st_mtime_ns),
}
SHELVE_CACHE_DIR = Path(shelve_cache_directory)
SHELVE_CACHE_PATH = SHELVE_CACHE_DIR / shelve_cache_basename
if enable_shelve_cache:
    SHELVE_CACHE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    if clear_shelve_cache:
        with shelve.open(str(SHELVE_CACHE_PATH)) as cache_db:
            cache_db.clear()

CSV_COLUMNS = [
    "PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER",
    "STUDY_USER_ID",
    "time_utc",
    "date_utc",
    "hour_of_day_utc",
    "fitbitSteps",
    "fitbitHRAvg",
    "fitbitSleepMinutes",
    "moodScore",
    "messageSent",
]

def normalize_timestamp_to_utc(value: str) -> pd.Timestamp:
    """Parse a timestamp string and ensure it is UTC-aware."""
    timestamp = pd.Timestamp(value)
    if timestamp.tzinfo is None:
        return timestamp.tz_localize("UTC")
    return timestamp.tz_convert("UTC")


def _cache_key_default(value):
    if isinstance(value, pd.Timestamp):
        return value.isoformat()
    if isinstance(value, Path):
        return str(value)
    if isinstance(value, (np.integer, np.floating)):
        return value.item()
    if isinstance(value, tuple):
        return list(value)
    raise TypeError(f"Object of type {type(value).__name__} is not JSON serializable.")


def build_cache_key(cache_name: str, **cache_inputs) -> str:
    payload = {
        "cache_name": cache_name,
        "cache_version_token": cache_version_token,
        "csv_signature": CSV_CACHE_SIGNATURE,
        "cache_inputs": cache_inputs,
    }
    encoded = json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, default=_cache_key_default).encode("utf-8")
    return f"{cache_name}:{hashlib.sha256(encoded).hexdigest()}"


def get_or_compute_shelve(cache_name: str, cache_inputs: dict, compute_fn, *, prepare_for_store=None):
    """Read a value from the shelve cache or compute and store it."""
    cache_key = build_cache_key(cache_name, **cache_inputs)
    if enable_shelve_cache:
        with shelve.open(str(SHELVE_CACHE_PATH)) as cache_db:
            if cache_key in cache_db:
                return cache_db[cache_key]
    value = compute_fn()
    stored_value = prepare_for_store(value) if prepare_for_store is not None else value
    if enable_shelve_cache:
        with shelve.open(str(SHELVE_CACHE_PATH)) as cache_db:
            cache_db[cache_key] = stored_value
    return stored_value


def display_table(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    *,
    precision: int = 3,
    formatters: dict | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Render tables without row indexes in the compiled document."""
    styled = df.style.hide(axis="index")
    if formatters is not None:
        styled = styled.format(formatters, na_rep="")
    else:
        styled = styled.format(precision=precision, na_rep="")
    display(styled)


def format_table_value(value, precision: int = 3) -> str:
    """Format table values compactly for LaTeX output."""
    if pd.isna(value):
        return ""
    if isinstance(value, (int, np.integer)):
        return str(int(value))
    if isinstance(value, (float, np.floating)):
        numeric_value = float(value)
        if np.isfinite(numeric_value) and abs(numeric_value - round(numeric_value)) < 1e-9:
            return str(int(round(numeric_value)))
        return f"{numeric_value:.{precision}f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".")
    return str(value)


def dataframe_to_latex_tabular(df: pd.DataFrame, *, precision: int = 3) -> str:
    """Convert a dataframe into a compact LaTeX tabular block."""
    formatted = df.copy()
    for column in formatted.columns:
        formatted[column] = formatted[column].map(lambda value: format_table_value(value, precision))
    return formatted.to_latex(index=False, escape=True)


def render_side_by_side_tables_latex(table_specs: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Render several small tables side by side using LaTeX minipages."""
    rendered_blocks = []
    for spec in table_specs:
        tabular_latex = dataframe_to_latex_tabular(spec["df"], precision=spec.get("precision", 3))
        rendered_blocks.append(
            "\n".join(
                [
                    rf"\begin{{minipage}}[t]{{{spec['width']}\linewidth}}",
                    r"\vspace{0pt}",
                    r"\centering",
                    rf"\captionof{{table}}{{{spec['caption']}}}",
                    r"\scriptsize",
                    tabular_latex,
                    r"\end{minipage}",
                ]
            )
        )
    return "\n\\hfill\n".join(rendered_blocks)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CovariateSpec:
    key: str
    label: str
    source_col: str
    beta_param: str
    description: str
    standardize: bool = True


COVARIATE_SPECS = {
    "trail_steps_24h": CovariateSpec(
        key="trail_steps_24h",
        label="Trailing 24h steps",
        source_col="cov_trail_steps_24h",
        beta_param="beta_steps24",
        description="Sum of Fitbit steps over the prior 24 hours.",
    ),
    "trail_sleep_minutes_24h": CovariateSpec(
        key="trail_sleep_minutes_24h",
        label="Trailing 24h sleep minutes",
        source_col="cov_trail_sleep_minutes_24h",
        beta_param="beta_sleep24",
        description="Sum of Fitbit sleep minutes over the prior 24 hours.",
    ),
    "trail_hr_mean_24h": CovariateSpec(
        key="trail_hr_mean_24h",
        label="Trailing 24h heart rate mean",
        source_col="cov_trail_hr_mean_24h",
        beta_param="beta_hr24",
        description="Average Fitbit heart rate over the prior 24 hours.",
    ),
    "trail_mood_mean_24h": CovariateSpec(
        key="trail_mood_mean_24h",
        label="Trailing 24h mood mean",
        source_col="cov_trail_mood_mean_24h",
        beta_param="beta_mood24",
        description="Average mood score over the prior 24 hours.",
    ),
    "send_lag_0_3h": CovariateSpec(
        key="send_lag_0_3h",
        label="Message sent 0 to 3 hours ago",
        source_col="cov_send_lag_0_3h",
        beta_param="eta_send_0_3h",
        description="Indicator that the most recent message was in the current hour or previous 3 hours.",
        standardize=False,
    ),
    "send_lag_4_11h": CovariateSpec(
        key="send_lag_4_11h",
        label="Message sent 4 to 11 hours ago",
        source_col="cov_send_lag_4_11h",
        beta_param="eta_send_4_11h",
        description="Indicator that the most recent message was 4 to 11 hours ago.",
        standardize=False,
    ),
    "send_lag_12_23h": CovariateSpec(
        key="send_lag_12_23h",
        label="Message sent 12 to 23 hours ago",
        source_col="cov_send_lag_12_23h",
        beta_param="eta_send_12_23h",
        description="Indicator that the most recent message was 12 to 23 hours ago.",
        standardize=False,
    ),
}


@dataclass
class HourlyStepModelData:
    participant_id: str
    study_user_id: str | None
    hourly_frame: pd.DataFrame
    covariate_keys: list[str]
    covariate_params: dict[str, str]
    covariate_standardization: dict[str, dict[str, float]]
    decision_window_frame: pd.DataFrame
    dropped_covariates: list[str]
    start_utc: pd.Timestamp
    end_utc: pd.Timestamp
    assumed_timezone: str

    @property
    def model_covariate_columns(self) -> list[str]:
        return [COVARIATE_SPECS[key].source_col for key in self.covariate_keys]

    @property
    def ys(self) -> pd.DataFrame:
        return self.hourly_frame.set_index("time_index")[["fitbit_steps_obs"]]

    @property
    def covars(self) -> pd.DataFrame:
        columns = ["baseline_log_mean"] + self.model_covariate_columns
        return self.hourly_frame.set_index("time_index")[columns]


@dataclass
class StepPompFitResult:
    initial_params: dict[str, float]
    fitted_params: dict[str, float]
    loglik: float
    pomp_object: Pomp | None
    mif_summary: pd.DataFrame
    mif_traces: pd.DataFrame
    pfilter_summary: pd.DataFrame
    timing_frame: pd.DataFrame
    runtime_breakdown: pd.DataFrame


@dataclass
class StepPompRunResult:
    initial_params: dict[str, float]
    fitted_params: dict[str, float]
    loglik: float
    pomp_object: Pomp | None
    mif_summary: pd.DataFrame
    mif_traces: pd.DataFrame
    pfilter_summary: pd.DataFrame
    filtered_states: pd.DataFrame
    plot_frame: pd.DataFrame
    reward_frame: pd.DataFrame
    timing_frame: pd.DataFrame
    runtime_breakdown: pd.DataFrame


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class GlobalSearchStageConfig:
    name: str
    n_starts: int
    keep_top: int
    particles: int
    mif_iterations: int
    cooling_fraction: float
    rw_sd_scale: float
    evaluation_particles: int
    evaluation_pfilter_reps: int
    n_fresh_random_starts: int = 0
    n_jittered_restarts: int = 0


@dataclass
class GlobalSearchResult:
    best_fit: StepPompFitResult
    best_run: StepPompRunResult
    candidate_frame: pd.DataFrame
    stage_summary: pd.DataFrame
    start_box_frame: pd.DataFrame
    total_runtime_seconds: float


def clone_hourly_step_model_data(data: HourlyStepModelData) -> HourlyStepModelData:
    return copy.deepcopy(data)


def _copy_frame(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    return frame.copy(deep=True)


def strip_step_pomp_fit_result(result: StepPompFitResult) -> StepPompFitResult:
    return StepPompFitResult(
        initial_params=copy.deepcopy(result.initial_params),
        fitted_params=copy.deepcopy(result.fitted_params),
        loglik=float(result.loglik),
        pomp_object=None,
        mif_summary=_copy_frame(result.mif_summary),
        mif_traces=_copy_frame(result.mif_traces),
        pfilter_summary=_copy_frame(result.pfilter_summary),
        timing_frame=_copy_frame(result.timing_frame),
        runtime_breakdown=_copy_frame(result.runtime_breakdown),
    )


def strip_step_pomp_run_result(result: StepPompRunResult) -> StepPompRunResult:
    return StepPompRunResult(
        initial_params=copy.deepcopy(result.initial_params),
        fitted_params=copy.deepcopy(result.fitted_params),
        loglik=float(result.loglik),
        pomp_object=None,
        mif_summary=_copy_frame(result.mif_summary),
        mif_traces=_copy_frame(result.mif_traces),
        pfilter_summary=_copy_frame(result.pfilter_summary),
        filtered_states=_copy_frame(result.filtered_states),
        plot_frame=_copy_frame(result.plot_frame),
        reward_frame=_copy_frame(result.reward_frame),
        timing_frame=_copy_frame(result.timing_frame),
        runtime_breakdown=_copy_frame(result.runtime_breakdown),
    )


def strip_global_search_result(result: GlobalSearchResult) -> GlobalSearchResult:
    return GlobalSearchResult(
        best_fit=strip_step_pomp_fit_result(result.best_fit),
        best_run=strip_step_pomp_run_result(result.best_run),
        candidate_frame=_copy_frame(result.candidate_frame),
        stage_summary=_copy_frame(result.stage_summary),
        start_box_frame=_copy_frame(result.start_box_frame),
        total_runtime_seconds=float(result.total_runtime_seconds),
    )


analysis_start = normalize_timestamp_to_utc(analysis_start_utc_iso)
analysis_end = normalize_timestamp_to_utc(analysis_end_utc_iso)
covariate_keys = list(participant_covariate_keys)
free_params = list(estimated_free_params)

initial_param_overrides = {
    "phi": float(initial_phi),
    "sigma": float(initial_sigma),
    "k_fitbit": float(initial_k_fitbit),
}

local_if2_controls = {
    "particles": int(local_mle_particles),
    "mif_iterations": int(local_mle_mif_iterations),
    "random_seed": int(local_mle_random_seed),
    "cooling_fraction": float(local_mle_cooling_fraction),
    "rw_sd_scale": float(local_mle_rw_sd_scale),
    "evaluation_particles": int(local_mle_evaluation_particles),
    "evaluation_pfilter_reps": int(local_mle_evaluation_pfilter_reps),
}

global_search_start_box = {
    "phi": (float(global_start_box_phi_low), float(global_start_box_phi_high)),
    "sigma": (float(global_start_box_sigma_low), float(global_start_box_sigma_high)),
    "k_fitbit": (float(global_start_box_k_fitbit_low), float(global_start_box_k_fitbit_high)),
}

global_search_controls = {
    "random_seed": int(global_search_random_seed),
    "include_current_theta_as_start": bool(global_search_include_current_theta_as_start),
    "jitter_scale": float(global_search_jitter_scale),
    "box_shrink_factor_per_stage": float(global_search_box_shrink_factor_per_stage),
}

global_search_stage_controls = [
    {
        "name": str(global_stage1_name),
        "n_starts": int(global_stage1_n_starts),
        "keep_top": int(global_stage1_keep_top),
        "particles": int(global_stage1_particles),
        "mif_iterations": int(global_stage1_mif_iterations),
        "cooling_fraction": float(global_stage1_cooling_fraction),
        "rw_sd_scale": float(global_stage1_rw_sd_scale),
        "evaluation_particles": int(global_stage1_evaluation_particles),
        "evaluation_pfilter_reps": int(global_stage1_evaluation_pfilter_reps),
        "n_fresh_random_starts": int(global_stage1_n_fresh_random_starts),
        "n_jittered_restarts": int(global_stage1_n_jittered_restarts),
    },
    {
        "name": str(global_stage2_name),
        "n_starts": int(global_stage2_n_starts),
        "keep_top": int(global_stage2_keep_top),
        "particles": int(global_stage2_particles),
        "mif_iterations": int(global_stage2_mif_iterations),
        "cooling_fraction": float(global_stage2_cooling_fraction),
        "rw_sd_scale": float(global_stage2_rw_sd_scale),
        "evaluation_particles": int(global_stage2_evaluation_particles),
        "evaluation_pfilter_reps": int(global_stage2_evaluation_pfilter_reps),
        "n_fresh_random_starts": int(global_stage2_n_fresh_random_starts),
        "n_jittered_restarts": int(global_stage2_n_jittered_restarts),
    },
]

local_controls_display = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        [
            {
                "Particles": local_if2_controls["particles"],
                "IF2 iterations": local_if2_controls["mif_iterations"],
                "Cooling fraction": local_if2_controls["cooling_fraction"],
                "RW SD scale": local_if2_controls["rw_sd_scale"],
                "Evaluation particles": local_if2_controls["evaluation_particles"],
                "Evaluation pfilter reps": local_if2_controls["evaluation_pfilter_reps"],
            }
        ],
        index=["Local"],
    )
    .transpose()
    .reset_index()
)
local_controls_display.columns = ["Setting", "Local"]

global_stage_controls_display = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        [
            {
                "Stage": stage["name"],
                "Starts": stage["n_starts"],
                "Keep top": stage["keep_top"],
                "Particles": stage["particles"],
                "IF2 iterations": stage["mif_iterations"],
                "Cooling fraction": stage["cooling_fraction"],
                "RW SD scale": stage["rw_sd_scale"],
                "Evaluation particles": stage["evaluation_particles"],
                "Evaluation pfilter reps": stage["evaluation_pfilter_reps"],
                "Fresh random starts": stage["n_fresh_random_starts"],
                "Jittered restarts": stage["n_jittered_restarts"],
            }
            for stage in global_search_stage_controls
        ]
    )
    .set_index("Stage")
    .transpose()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"index": "Setting"})
)
global_start_box_display = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "Parameter": name,
            "Low": bounds[0],
            "High": bounds[1],
        }
        for name, bounds in global_search_start_box.items()
    ]
)

print(
    render_side_by_side_tables_latex(
        [
            {
                "caption": "Local IF2 controls",
                "width": 0.27,
                "df": local_controls_display,
                "precision": 3,
            },
            {
                "caption": "Global IF2 stage controls",
                "width": 0.43,
                "df": global_stage_controls_display,
                "precision": 3,
            },
            {
                "caption": "Global start box",
                "width": 0.24,
                "df": global_start_box_display,
                "precision": 3,
            },
        ]
    )
)
```
paragraph 1: filter for individuals with low missing rates

```{python}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false

# @lru_cache(maxsize=8)
# def load_participant_hourly_from_csv(
#     participant_id: str,
#     start_iso: str,
#     end_iso: str,
#     lookback_hours: int = 48,
# ) -> pd.DataFrame:
#     """Load one participant from the CSV with a short lookback window."""

#     start_utc = pd.Timestamp(start_iso).tz_convert("UTC") - pd.Timedelta(hours=lookback_hours)
#     end_utc = pd.Timestamp(end_iso).tz_convert("UTC")
#     chunks = []

#     for chunk in pd.read_csv(CSV_PATH, usecols=CSV_COLUMNS, chunksize=250_000):
#         chunk = chunk.loc[chunk["PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER"] == participant_id].copy()
#         if chunk.empty:
#             continue

#         chunk["time_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(chunk["time_utc"], utc=True, errors="coerce")
#         chunk = chunk.loc[chunk["time_utc"].between(start_utc, end_utc, inclusive="both")]
#         if chunk.empty:
#             continue

#         chunk["date_utc"] = chunk["time_utc"].dt.date
#         chunk["hour_of_day_utc"] = chunk["time_utc"].dt.hour
#         chunks.append(chunk)

#     if not chunks:
#         raise ValueError(f"No hourly rows were found for {participant_id}.")

#     return pd.concat(chunks, ignore_index=True).sort_values("time_utc").reset_index(drop=True)


# def build_baseline_log_mean(
#     step_proxy: pd.Series,
#     local_hour: pd.Series,
#     local_dayofweek: pd.Series,
# ) -> pd.Series:
#     """Empirical hour-of-day and weekday mean structure on the log scale."""

#     log_proxy = np.log(step_proxy.clip(lower=0.0) + 1.0)
#     valid = log_proxy.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan).dropna()
#     global_mean = float(valid.mean()) if not valid.empty else float(np.log(1.0))

#     hour_effect = (
#         log_proxy.groupby(local_hour).mean().reindex(range(24), fill_value=np.nan).astype(float) - global_mean
#     )
#     hour_effect = hour_effect.fillna(0.0)
#     hour_effect = hour_effect - float(hour_effect.mean())

#     hour_lookup = hour_effect.reindex(local_hour.astype(int)).to_numpy(dtype=float)
#     residual = log_proxy.to_numpy(dtype=float) - (global_mean + hour_lookup)
#     residual_series = pd.Series(residual, index=step_proxy.index)

#     dow_effect = residual_series.groupby(local_dayofweek).mean().reindex(range(7), fill_value=np.nan).astype(float)
#     dow_effect = dow_effect.fillna(0.0)
#     dow_effect = dow_effect - float(dow_effect.mean())

#     baseline = (
#         global_mean
#         + hour_effect.reindex(local_hour.astype(int)).to_numpy(dtype=float)
#         + dow_effect.reindex(local_dayofweek.astype(int)).to_numpy(dtype=float)
#     )
#     return pd.Series(baseline, index=step_proxy.index, dtype="float64")


# def add_hourly_covariates(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
#     """Create trailing summaries and message-lag indicators from the CSV."""

#     result = frame.copy()
#     step_proxy = pd.to_numeric(result["fitbitSteps"], errors="coerce").clip(lower=0.0)
#     sleep_minutes = pd.to_numeric(result.get("fitbitSleepMinutes"), errors="coerce")
#     hr_avg = pd.to_numeric(result.get("fitbitHRAvg"), errors="coerce")
#     mood_score = pd.to_numeric(result.get("moodScore"), errors="coerce")

#     hr_fill = float(hr_avg.dropna().median()) if hr_avg.notna().any() else 0.0
#     mood_fill = float(mood_score.dropna().mean()) if mood_score.notna().any() else 0.0

#     result["cov_trail_steps_24h"] = step_proxy.fillna(0.0).shift(1).rolling(24, min_periods=1).sum()
#     result["cov_trail_sleep_minutes_24h"] = sleep_minutes.fillna(0.0).shift(1).rolling(24, min_periods=1).sum()
#     result["cov_trail_hr_mean_24h"] = hr_avg.fillna(hr_fill).shift(1).rolling(24, min_periods=1).mean()
#     result["cov_trail_mood_mean_24h"] = mood_score.fillna(mood_fill).shift(1).rolling(24, min_periods=1).mean()

#     send_series = pd.to_numeric(result.get("messageSent", 0), errors="coerce").fillna(0.0).clip(lower=0.0, upper=1.0)
#     hours_since_send = np.full(len(result), np.inf, dtype=float)
#     last_send_index = None
#     for index, sent_flag in enumerate(send_series.to_numpy(dtype=float)):
#         if sent_flag >= 0.5:
#             last_send_index = index
#             hours_since_send[index] = 0.0
#         elif last_send_index is not None:
#             hours_since_send[index] = float(index - last_send_index)

#     result["cov_send_lag_0_3h"] = ((hours_since_send >= 0) & (hours_since_send <= 3)).astype(float)
#     result["cov_send_lag_4_11h"] = ((hours_since_send >= 4) & (hours_since_send <= 11)).astype(float)
#     result["cov_send_lag_12_23h"] = ((hours_since_send >= 12) & (hours_since_send <= 23)).astype(float)
#     result["baseline_log_mean"] = build_baseline_log_mean(
#         step_proxy,
#         result["local_hour"],
#         result["local_dayofweek"],
#     )
#     return result


# def build_decision_windows(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
#     """Identify local 3pm decision times and their following 24-hour windows."""

#     decision_rows = frame.loc[frame["local_hour"] == 15].copy()
#     if decision_rows.empty:
#         return pd.DataFrame(
#             columns=[
#                 "decision_id",
#                 "decision_time_utc",
#                 "decision_local_time",
#                 "decision_local_date",
#                 "hours_in_window",
#                 "complete_window",
#                 "fitbit_observed_hours",
#                 "fitbit_coverage_fraction",
#                 "reward_window_status",
#                 "window_start_position",
#                 "window_end_position",
#             ]
#         )

#     rows = []
#     for decision_id, (_, row) in enumerate(decision_rows.iterrows(), start=1):
#         start_pos = int(row.name)
#         window = frame.iloc[start_pos : start_pos + 24].copy()
#         hours_in_window = len(window)
#         complete_window = hours_in_window == 24
#         fitbit_observed = int(window["fitbit_observed"].sum())
#         fitbit_coverage_fraction = fitbit_observed / hours_in_window if hours_in_window > 0 else 0.0

#         if not complete_window:
#             status = "truncated_range"
#         elif fitbit_observed >= 24:
#             status = "fully_observed"
#         elif fitbit_coverage_fraction >= 0.5:
#             status = "partially_observed"
#         else:
#             status = "heavily_imputed"

#         rows.append(
#             {
#                 "decision_id": decision_id,
#                 "decision_time_utc": row["time_utc"],
#                 "decision_local_time": row["local_time"],
#                 "decision_local_date": row["local_date"],
#                 "hours_in_window": hours_in_window,
#                 "complete_window": bool(complete_window),
#                 "fitbit_observed_hours": fitbit_observed,
#                 "fitbit_coverage_fraction": fitbit_coverage_fraction,
#                 "reward_window_status": status,
#                 "window_start_position": start_pos,
#                 "window_end_position": start_pos + hours_in_window - 1,
#             }
#         )

#     return pd.DataFrame(rows)


# def prepare_hourly_model_data(
#     hourly_frame: pd.DataFrame,
#     *,
#     participant_id: str,
#     start_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     end_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     covariate_keys: list[str],
#     assumed_timezone: str,
# ) -> HourlyStepModelData:
#     """Prepare the participant-specific data object used by the POMP model."""

#     if hourly_frame.empty:
#         raise ValueError("Selected participant/date range produced no hourly rows.")

#     working = hourly_frame.copy()
#     working["time_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(working["time_utc"], utc=True, errors="coerce")
#     working = working.dropna(subset=["time_utc"]).sort_values("time_utc").reset_index(drop=True)

#     study_user_id = None
#     if "STUDY_USER_ID" in working.columns:
#         values = working["STUDY_USER_ID"].dropna().astype(str)
#         study_user_id = values.iloc[0] if not values.empty else None

#     working["local_time"] = working["time_utc"].dt.tz_convert(assumed_timezone)
#     working["local_date"] = working["local_time"].dt.date
#     working["local_hour"] = working["local_time"].dt.hour.astype(int)
#     working["local_dayofweek"] = working["local_time"].dt.dayofweek.astype(int)
#     working = add_hourly_covariates(working)

#     selected = working.loc[(working["time_utc"] >= start_utc) & (working["time_utc"] <= end_utc)].copy()
#     if selected.empty:
#         raise ValueError("No hourly rows fall inside the requested UTC range.")

#     selected["fitbit_steps_obs"] = pd.to_numeric(selected["fitbitSteps"], errors="coerce")
#     selected["fitbit_observed"] = selected["fitbit_steps_obs"].notna().astype(int)
#     if int(selected["fitbit_observed"].sum()) == 0:
#         raise ValueError("No observed Fitbit step data are available in the selected range.")

#     normalized_covariates = []
#     dropped_covariates = []
#     covariate_standardization = {}
#     covariate_params = {}

#     for key in covariate_keys:
#         if key not in COVARIATE_SPECS:
#             continue
#         spec = COVARIATE_SPECS[key]
#         values = pd.to_numeric(selected[spec.source_col], errors="coerce")
#         if spec.standardize:
#             mean = float(values.mean()) if values.notna().any() else 0.0
#             std = float(values.std(ddof=0)) if values.notna().any() else 0.0
#             if np.isclose(std, 0.0):
#                 standardized = values.fillna(mean) - mean
#             else:
#                 standardized = (values.fillna(mean) - mean) / std
#             if float(np.nanmax(np.abs(standardized.to_numpy(dtype=float)))) <= 1e-12:
#                 dropped_covariates.append(key)
#                 continue
#             selected[spec.source_col] = standardized.astype(float)
#             covariate_standardization[key] = {"mean": mean, "std": std}
#         else:
#             binary_values = values.fillna(0.0).astype(float)
#             if float(binary_values.max()) <= 0.0:
#                 dropped_covariates.append(key)
#                 continue
#             selected[spec.source_col] = binary_values
#             covariate_standardization[key] = {"mean": 0.0, "std": 1.0}

#         covariate_params[key] = spec.beta_param
#         normalized_covariates.append(key)

#     selected = selected.sort_values("time_utc").reset_index(drop=True)
#     selected["time_index"] = np.arange(1, len(selected) + 1, dtype=float)
#     decision_window_frame = build_decision_windows(selected)

#     return HourlyStepModelData(
#         participant_id=participant_id,
#         study_user_id=study_user_id,
#         hourly_frame=selected,
#         covariate_keys=normalized_covariates,
#         covariate_params=covariate_params,
#         covariate_standardization=covariate_standardization,
#         decision_window_frame=decision_window_frame,
#         dropped_covariates=dropped_covariates,
#         start_utc=start_utc,
#         end_utc=end_utc,
#         assumed_timezone=assumed_timezone,
#     )


# def build_step_pomp_default_params(hourly_data: HourlyStepModelData) -> dict[str, float]:
#     defaults = {
#         "phi": 0.75,
#         "sigma": 0.30,
#         "k_fitbit": 10,
#     }
#     for cov_key in hourly_data.covariate_keys:
#         defaults[COVARIATE_SPECS[cov_key].beta_param] = 0.0
#     return defaults


# def get_cached_participant_hourly(
#     participant_id: str,
#     *,
#     start_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     end_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     lookback_hours: int,
# ) -> pd.DataFrame:
#     cache_inputs = {
#         "participant_id": participant_id,
#         "analysis_start_utc_iso": start_utc.isoformat(),
#         "analysis_end_utc_iso": end_utc.isoformat(),
#         "lookback_hours": int(lookback_hours),
#     }
#     return get_or_compute_shelve(
#         "participant_hourly_frame",
#         cache_inputs,
#         lambda: load_participant_hourly_from_csv(
#             participant_id,
#             start_utc.isoformat(),
#             end_utc.isoformat(),
#             lookback_hours=lookback_hours,
#         ),
#         prepare_for_store=lambda frame: frame.copy(deep=True),
#     )


# def get_cached_hourly_model_data(
#     participant_hourly: pd.DataFrame,
#     *,
#     participant_id: str,
#     start_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     end_utc: pd.Timestamp,
#     covariate_keys: list[str],
#     assumed_timezone: str,
# ) -> HourlyStepModelData:
#     cache_inputs = {
#         "participant_id": participant_id,
#         "analysis_start_utc_iso": start_utc.isoformat(),
#         "analysis_end_utc_iso": end_utc.isoformat(),
#         "covariate_keys": list(covariate_keys),
#         "assumed_timezone": assumed_timezone,
#     }
#     return get_or_compute_shelve(
#         "participant_hourly_model_data",
#         cache_inputs,
#         lambda: prepare_hourly_model_data(
#             participant_hourly,
#             participant_id=participant_id,
#             start_utc=start_utc,
#             end_utc=end_utc,
#             covariate_keys=covariate_keys,
#             assumed_timezone=assumed_timezone,
#         ),
#         prepare_for_store=clone_hourly_step_model_data,
#     )

# participant_hourly = get_cached_participant_hourly(
#     participant_id,
#     start_utc=analysis_start,
#     end_utc=analysis_end,
#     lookback_hours=participant_data_lookback_hours,
# )

# hourly_data = get_cached_hourly_model_data(
#     participant_hourly,
#     participant_id=participant_id,
#     start_utc=analysis_start,
#     end_utc=analysis_end,
#     covariate_keys=covariate_keys,
#     assumed_timezone=assumed_local_timezone,
# )

# initial_params = build_step_pomp_default_params(hourly_data)
# for param_name, param_value in initial_param_overrides.items():
#     if param_name in initial_params:
#         initial_params[param_name] = param_value

# active_free_params = [param for param in free_params if param in initial_params]

# fitbit_missingness_percentage = 100.0 * (
#     1.0 - float(hourly_data.hourly_frame["fitbit_observed"].mean())
# )

# participant_summary = pd.DataFrame(
#     [
#         {
#             "Participant ID": participant_id,
#             "Complete 24 hour reward windows": int(hourly_data.decision_window_frame["complete_window"].sum()),
#             "Fitbit missingness percentage": f"{fitbit_missingness_percentage:.2f}%",
#         }
#     ]
# )

# display_table(participant_summary, precision=2)
```




```{python}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: global-search

# can maybe delete?

# def _coerce_free_params(free_params: list[str] | None, initial_params: dict[str, float]) -> list[str]:

#         return list(initial_params.keys())
#     return [name for name in free_params if name in initial_params]


# def _coerce_start_box(
#     start_box: dict[str, tuple[float, float]] | None,

#     start_box = start_box or {}
#     for name in free_params:

#         if raw_bounds is None:
#             value = float(initial_params[name])
#             width = max(abs(value) * 0.25, 0.10)
#             result[name] = (value - width, value + width)
#             continue
#         low, high = float(raw_bounds[0]), float(raw_bounds[1])
#         if high < low:
#             low, high = high, low
#         if np.isclose(low, high):
#             eps = max(abs(low) * 0.01, 1e-3)
#             low, high = low - eps, high + eps
#         result[name] = (low, high)
#     return result


# def _build_start_box_frame(start_box: dict[str, tuple[float, float]]) -> pd.DataFrame:

#                 "start_high": bounds[1],
#                 "start_width": bounds[1] - bounds[0],
#             }
#             for name, bounds in sorted(start_box.items())

#     base_start_box: dict[str, tuple[float, float]],
#     center_params: dict[str, float],
#     *,
#     stage_index: int,
#     box_shrink_factor_per_stage: float,

#     result = {}
#     for name, (base_low, base_high) in base_start_box.items():

#             result[name] = (float(base_low), float(base_high))
#             continue
#         width = float(base_high - base_low) * shrink_factor
#         center = float(np.clip(center_params.get(name, 0.5 * (base_low + base_high)), base_low, base_high))
#         low = max(float(base_low), center - 0.5 * width)
#         high = min(float(base_high), center + 0.5 * width)
#         if np.isclose(low, high):
#             eps = max(abs(center) * 0.01, 1e-3)
#             low = max(float(base_low), center - eps)
#             high = min(float(base_high), center + eps)
#         result[name] = (float(low), float(high))
#     return result


# def _sample_uniform_start(
#     template_params: dict[str, float],
#     free_params: list[str],

#     for name in free_params:

#         params[name] = float(rng.uniform(low, high))

#         low, high = bounds[name]

# def _build_stage_candidates(
#     *,
#     stage_index: int,
#     stage: GlobalSearchStageConfig,
#     template_params: dict[str, float],
#     free_params: list[str],

#                 {

#     survivors = previous_survivors[:n_starts]

#                 "parent_candidate_id": survivor["candidate_id"],

#         anchor = previous_survivors[int(rng.integers(0, len(previous_survivors)))]

#                     free_params,

#                     rng,
#                     jitter_scale=jitter_scale,

#     remaining = n_starts - len(candidates)

# def run_multistage_step_pomp_search(
#     data: HourlyStepModelData,
#     *,
#     initial_params: dict[str, float],
#     free_params: list[str] | None,
#     start_box: dict[str, tuple[float, float]] | None,
#     stage_configs: list[GlobalSearchStageConfig],
#     random_seed: int,
#     include_current_theta_as_start: bool = True,
#     jitter_scale: float = 0.10,
#     box_shrink_factor_per_stage: float = 1.0,
# ) -> GlobalSearchResult:
#     normalized_initial = {name: float(value) for name, value in initial_params.items()}
#     normalized_free_params = _coerce_free_params(free_params, normalized_initial)
#     if not normalized_free_params:
#         raise ValueError("Select at least one parameter to estimate in the global MLE search.")
#     if not stage_configs:
#         raise ValueError("Provide at least one stage for the global search.")

#     normalized_start_box = _coerce_start_box(start_box, normalized_free_params, normalized_initial)
#     start_box_frame = _build_start_box_frame(normalized_start_box)

#     previous_survivors = []
#     current_center = dict(normalized_initial)
#     best_fit = None
#     best_candidate_row = None
#     all_rows = []
#     stage_summary_rows = []
#     total_runtime_seconds = 0.0

#     for stage_index, stage in enumerate(stage_configs):

#             current_center,
#             stage_index=stage_index,
#             box_shrink_factor_per_stage=box_shrink_factor_per_stage,

#             stage_index=stage_index,
#             stage=stage,

#             rng=rng,
#         )

#         stage_rows = []
#         for candidate_index, candidate in enumerate(stage_candidates, start=1):
#             candidate_id = f"stage_{stage_index + 1:02d}_cand_{candidate_index:03d}"

#             fit_start = perf_counter()
#             fitted_params = {}
#             fit_initial_params = dict(candidate["params"])
#             loglik = float("nan")
#             error_text = ""
#             fit_succeeded = False
#             fit_result = None

#             try:
#                 fit_result = run_step_pomp_fit(
#                     data,
#                     initial_params=fit_initial_params,
#                     free_params=normalized_free_params,
#                     particles=stage.particles,
#                     mif_iterations=stage.mif_iterations,
#                     random_seed=fit_seed,
#                     cooling_fraction=stage.cooling_fraction,
#                     rw_sd_scale=stage.rw_sd_scale,
#                     evaluation_particles=stage.evaluation_particles,
#                     evaluation_pfilter_reps=stage.evaluation_pfilter_reps,

#                 loglik = float(fit_result.loglik)

#                 fit_initial_params = dict(fit_result.initial_params)

#                     np.isfinite(loglik)
#                     and (not np.isfinite(best_fit.loglik) or loglik > float(best_fit.loglik))

#                     best_fit = fit_result

#                 error_text = str(exc)

#             runtime_seconds = float(perf_counter() - fit_start)
#             total_runtime_seconds += runtime_seconds

#             row = {
#                 "stage_index": stage_index + 1,
#                 "stage_name": stage.name,
#                 "candidate_id": candidate_id,
#                 "parent_candidate_id": candidate["parent_candidate_id"],
#                 "origin": str(candidate["origin"]),
#                 "fit_seed": fit_seed,
#                 "fit_succeeded": fit_succeeded,
#                 "loglik": loglik,
#                 "runtime_seconds": runtime_seconds,
#                 "particles": stage.particles,

#                 "evaluation_particles": stage.evaluation_particles,
#                 "evaluation_pfilter_reps": stage.evaluation_pfilter_reps,

#                 row[f"start_{name}"] = float(fit_initial_params.get(name, normalized_initial[name]))

#             if fit_succeeded and fit_result is not None:

#                     best_candidate_row = dict(row)

#         stage_frame = pd.DataFrame(stage_rows)

#         successful_frame = stage_frame.loc[stage_frame["fit_succeeded"]].copy()

#         if previous_survivors:
#             current_center = dict(previous_survivors[0]["fitted_params"])

#         stage_summary_rows.append(
#             {

#                 "stage_name": stage.name,
#                 "n_starts": stage.n_starts,
#                 "n_successful": int(successful_frame["fit_succeeded"].sum()) if "fit_succeeded" in successful_frame.columns else int(len(successful_frame)),
#                 "keep_top": stage.keep_top,
#                 "best_loglik": float(pd.to_numeric(stage_frame["loglik"], errors="coerce").max()) if stage_frame["loglik"].notna().any() else np.nan,
#                 "median_loglik": float(pd.to_numeric(successful_frame["loglik"], errors="coerce").median()) if not successful_frame.empty else np.nan,
#                 "runtime_seconds": float(pd.to_numeric(stage_frame["runtime_seconds"], errors="coerce").sum()),

#                 "stage_best_candidate_id": str(stage_frame.iloc[0]["candidate_id"]),
#             }
#         )

#     if best_fit is None or best_candidate_row is None:
#         raise ValueError("No successful IF2 fits were produced in the global search.")

#     best_run = run_step_pomp_if2(
#         data,
#         initial_params=dict(best_fit.fitted_params),
#         free_params=[],
#         particles=int(best_candidate_row["evaluation_particles"]),

# def get_cached_global_search_result(
#     data: HourlyStepModelData,
#     *,
#     participant_id: str,
#     analysis_start: pd.Timestamp,

#     for stage_dict in global_search_stage_controls

```

```{python}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: coefficent-table

# Compare the starting values with the available fitted results. The local-fit
# column is included only when that chunk has been run in this document.
# local_fit_result = globals().get("local_step_pomp_result")
# coefficient_rows = []
# for param_name in initial_params:
#     global_value = global_best_fit.fitted_params.get(param_name, initial_params[param_name])
#     row = {
#         "Parameter": param_name,
#         "Initial value": initial_params[param_name],
#         "Global MLE value": global_value,
#     }
#     if local_fit_result is not None:
#         row["Local MLE value"] = local_fit_result.fitted_params.get(
#             param_name,
#             initial_params[param_name],
#         )
#     coefficient_rows.append(row)

# coefficient_table = pd.DataFrame(coefficient_rows).round(3)
#display_table(coefficient_table, precision=3)
```


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: load-script
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

import hw8_analysis as hw8
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: run-hw8
participant_ids = ["MDH-3531-4796", "MDH-4235-8718", "MDH-7960-3351"]

analysis_cfg = hw8.AnalysisConfig(
    analysis_start_utc_iso=analysis_start_utc_iso,
    analysis_end_utc_iso=analysis_end_utc_iso,
    participant_data_lookback_hours=participant_data_lookback_hours,
    assumed_local_timezone=assumed_local_timezone,
    participant_covariate_keys=list(participant_covariate_keys),
    estimated_free_params=list(estimated_free_params),
    initial_phi=initial_phi,
    initial_sigma=initial_sigma,
    initial_k_fitbit=initial_k_fitbit,
    local_mle_particles=local_mle_particles,
    local_mle_mif_iterations=local_mle_mif_iterations,
    local_mle_random_seed=local_mle_random_seed,
    local_mle_cooling_fraction=local_mle_cooling_fraction,
    local_mle_rw_sd_scale=local_mle_rw_sd_scale,
    local_mle_evaluation_particles=local_mle_evaluation_particles,
    local_mle_evaluation_pfilter_reps=local_mle_evaluation_pfilter_reps,
    global_search_random_seed=global_search_random_seed,
    global_search_include_current_theta_as_start=global_search_include_current_theta_as_start,
    global_search_jitter_scale=global_search_jitter_scale,
    global_search_box_shrink_factor_per_stage=global_search_box_shrink_factor_per_stage,
    global_start_box_phi_low=global_start_box_phi_low,
    global_start_box_phi_high=global_start_box_phi_high,
    global_start_box_sigma_low=global_start_box_sigma_low,
    global_start_box_sigma_high=global_start_box_sigma_high,
    global_start_box_k_fitbit_low=global_start_box_k_fitbit_low,
    global_start_box_k_fitbit_high=global_start_box_k_fitbit_high,
    global_search_stage_controls=list(global_search_stage_controls),
)

results = []
for pid in participant_ids:
    last4 = pid.split("-")[-1]
    cache_cfg = hw8.CacheConfig(
        enable_shelve_cache=enable_shelve_cache,
        clear_shelve_cache=clear_shelve_cache,
        cache_version_token=cache_version_token,
        shelve_cache_directory=shelve_cache_directory,
        shelve_cache_basename=f"example_{last4}",   # per participant
        csv_path=str(CSV_PATH),
    )
    results.append(hw8.run_participant(pid, cache_cfg=cache_cfg, cfg=analysis_cfg))
```

<!--
What things do we say ABOUT a global search? Could do scatterplots of parameters vs parameters (does big sigma get compensated for with small phi? these kinds of questions), potentially profile likelihoods.
-->

We chose three participants to illustrate how different levels of missingness affect the model fitting and reward prediction. @reward-plot demonstrates the difference between the latent and observed rewards for individual participants with high and medium missingness. We see that the estimated latent reward is always greater than the observed reward. There is a larger difference for the participant with high missingness. This is expected because it's a sum of hourly buckets, for which many of the observations are missing. @residual-plot shows that there's a linear trend in how much the estimated latent exceeds the observed. This is unsurprising; if the observed only sums 8 non-zero buckets, the latent sums 24 non-zero buckets, and might be approximately three times larger. This discrepancy motivates using a latent model, as we expect that there are many steps the participant is taking that aren't being picked up by Fitbit or other measurement devices. The missing data presents an additional challenge for benchmarking the accuracy of the model.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: concat-tables
# display tables

participant_summary_all = pd.concat([r.participant_summary for r in results], ignore_index=True)
participant_summary_all["Fitbit missingness percentage"] = participant_summary_all["Fitbit missingness percentage"].map(lambda x: f"{x:.2f}%")


local_mle_all = pd.concat([r.local_mle_summary for r in results], ignore_index=True).round(3)
global_best_all = pd.concat([r.global_best_summary for r in results], ignore_index=True).round(3)
coef_all = pd.concat([r.coefficient_table for r in results], ignore_index=True).round(3)
stage_all = pd.concat([r.global_stage_summary for r in results], ignore_index=True).round(3)

```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: tbl-participant-summary
# | tbl-cap: "Participant-level summary of data completeness and reward windows"

display_table(participant_summary_all, precision=2)

```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: tbl-global-mle
# | tbl-cap: "Summary of global search results"

display_table(global_best_all, precision=3)
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: reward-plot
def select_run(r: hw8.ParticipantResult, source: str) -> hw8.StepPompRunResult:
    s = str(source).lower().strip()
    if s == "local":
        return r.local_run
    return r.global_search_result.best_run

plot_source = reward_plot_fit_source  # "local" or "global"
n = 2 #len(results) # set to 1 for individual plots, or len(results) for all participants in one figure with subplots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=n, ncols=1, figsize=(reward_plot_fig_width, reward_plot_fig_height * n), sharex=False)

if n == 1:
    axes = [axes]

for ax, r in zip(axes, results):
    run = select_run(r, plot_source)
    reward_plot = run.reward_frame.copy()
    reward_plot = reward_plot.loc[reward_plot["complete_window"]].copy()
    reward_plot["decision_date"] = pd.to_datetime(reward_plot["decision_local_time"], errors="coerce").dt.tz_localize(None)

    ax.bar(reward_plot["decision_date"], reward_plot["latent_reward_24h"], color="#d69e2e", alpha=0.75, width=0.8, label="Filtered latent reward")
    ax.plot(reward_plot["decision_date"], reward_plot["fitbit_observed_reward_24h"], color="#2b6cb0", linewidth=1.6, marker="o", markersize=3, label="Observed Fitbit subtotal")

    ax.set_title(f"Reconstructed 24-hour reward ({plot_source}) — {r.participant_id}", fontsize=reward_plot_title_fontsize)
    ax.set_ylabel("24-hour step total", fontsize=reward_plot_axis_label_fontsize)
    ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=reward_plot_tick_fontsize)
    for label in ax.get_xticklabels():
        label.set_rotation(45)
        label.set_ha("right")

axes[-1].set_xlabel("Decision date", fontsize=reward_plot_axis_label_fontsize)
axes[0].legend(frameon=False, ncol=2, fontsize=reward_plot_legend_fontsize)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | label: residual-plot
# Residuals: all participants on one plot + per-participant trend line
plot_source = residual_plot_fit_source

# Color-blind friendly palette (Okabe–Ito)
palette = [
    "#0072B2",  # blue
    "#D55E00",  # vermillion
    "#009E73",  # bluish green
    "#CC79A7",  # reddish purple
    "#E69F00",  # orange
    "#56B4E9",  # sky blue
    "#F0E442",  # yellow (use sparingly on white)
    "#000000",  # black
]

plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(residual_plot_fig_width, residual_plot_fig_height))

ax.axhline(0.0, color="#4a5568", linewidth=1.2, linestyle="--")

for i, r in enumerate(results):
    color = palette[i % len(palette)]
    run = select_run(r, plot_source)

    df = run.reward_frame.copy()
    df = df.loc[df["complete_window"]].copy()

    x = (24 - pd.to_numeric(df["fitbit_observed_hours"], errors="coerce").fillna(0.0)).round().clip(0, 24)
    y = pd.to_numeric(df["latent_reward_24h"], errors="coerce") - pd.to_numeric(df["fitbit_observed_reward_24h"], errors="coerce")

    plot_df = pd.DataFrame({"x": x, "y": y}).dropna()
    plot_df["x"] = plot_df["x"].astype(int)

    # points
    ax.scatter(
        plot_df["x"],
        plot_df["y"],
        color=color,
        alpha=0.75,
        s=40,
        edgecolor="white",
        linewidth=0.5,
        label=r.participant_id,
    )

    # trend line (simple OLS y = a + b x), only if enough points
    if len(plot_df) >= 2 and plot_df["x"].nunique() >= 2:
        b, a = np.polyfit(plot_df["x"].to_numpy(dtype=float), plot_df["y"].to_numpy(dtype=float), deg=1)
        xs = np.linspace(0, 24, 200)
        ys = a + b * xs
        ax.plot(xs, ys, color=color, linewidth=2.0, alpha=0.95)

ax.set_title("Filtered reward residual vs missingness (by participant)")
ax.set_xlabel("Missing Fitbit hours in 24-hour window")
ax.set_ylabel("Filtered reward − observed subtotal")
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 24.5)
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(0, 25, 2))
ax.legend(frameon=False, ncol=1, fontsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# Benchmarks for Aggregate Modeling

Benchmark Draft:

There were several benchmarks to consider, and narrowing them down was challenging. Since our goal was prediction rather than inference, we prioritized models that perform best on unseen data. Specifically, we aimed to improve the accuracy of predicted 24-hour rewards when users have missing hourly observations. Additionally, we're fitting 78 personalized models jointly, so we need an aggregate benchmark that captures this predictive ability across all of the participants.

First, we considered evaluating error, defined as the difference between predicted and observed values, only for hourly observations with nonzero Fitbit step counts. Since participants do not always wear their devices, some observed step counts are recorded as zero. As you can see from the plot

Since error can only be meaningfully computed when the observed step count is greater than zero, we used a masking approach. This involves fitting two models: one on all observed hourly data and another on 80% of the observed data (with 20% masked). To compute error, we use the model trained on the 80% subset and evaluate it on the 20% of masked observations that originally had nonzero step counts. We refer to this performance metric as the masked subtotal. More information on how the metric was computed can be found in the supplementary material.

# Inference Settings and Computing Constraints(considerations)

Global search parameters used, and challenges of running panel pomp on 6 models, across 78 individuals.


# Results


Models we analyze:
ARMA(p, q), we pick the ARMA model which fits best by AIC
AR(1) + individual k
AR(1) + global pool k
AR(1) + partial pool k


Analysis of results:
Display by model, averaged across users:
log likelihood
Hourly RMSE
Reward RMSE
daily RMSE/sqrt(m)
hourly correlation with observation
daily correlation with observation


average phi, average sigma
average k, std k

3 scatter plots
phi vs sigma, sigma vs k, phi vs k

Each point is for a single participant after inference, not for a single iteration of filtering. Doesn't check convergence of filtering, looks at similarity between users, and potential parameter tradeoffs, after filtering.

# Conclusion

paragraph 1: summary of the analysis that was done

partial pooling did slightly better compared to individual with no pooling - suggesting shared structure
individiual
paritial normal distribution
globally shared k 

three maksed subtotal correlation, log likelihood

paragraph 2: limitations, direction for future analysis, wrap up sentence

Limitations:
Potentially benchmarking limitations. We would like to include more covariates such as heartrate and sleep. We ran into a limitation with pypomp that it doesn't easily support different feature sets for different participants, but mathematically a pomp model could do this and it would be useful here. We would like to model the measurement distribution for all three devices, because some users use multiple devices, and leveraging them jointly would be good. Participant clustering to pool parameters for participants who are similar (such as people who have similar sleep schedules). Models we didn't get to try. How much masking affects performance. Different ways of doing masking (contiguous, across whole days, totally random, etc).

Summary of Analysis Draft:

Our project focused on predicting the 24-hour reward for participants using Fitbit step count data. We compared three different approaches: an individual (no pooling) model, in which each participant has their own over-dispersion parameter $k$; a partial pooling model, in which each participant’s $k$ value is drawn from a shared distribution with a penalty term to prevent extreme deviations; and a global pooling model, in which all participants share the same $k$ value. Our results show that partial pooling performed slightly better than no pooling which suggests the presence of shared structure across participants.

The primary metric we used to evaluate how well the model, trained on 80\% of the observed hourly data, performed in prediction was the masked subtotal error. This metric measures the difference between predicted and observed values for the 20\% of data that was masked, but originally had observed step counts. Additionally, we computed the correlation between observed and predicted step counts at both the daily and hourly levels. The hourly correlation was 0.27, whereas the daily reward correlation was much stronger at 0.77. We also used log-likelihood as an additional evaluation measure.

Limitations Draft:

Due to time limitations, there are several additional methods we would have liked to explore in our model specification. For $$log \lambda_t$$, we would have liked to include additional covariates related to physical activity, such as heart rate and sleep. Our current latent process model only incorporates baseline averages over hour of day and week. Additionally, we were not able to model the measurement distribution jointly across all three devices for participants who used multiple devices, and therefore could not fully leverage all available data. Instead, we focused primarily on Fitbit data due to difficulties in disentangling device-specific biases. In terms of parameter sharing, we also considered clustering participants based on shared characteristics, such as similar sleep schedules, to better capture common behavioral patterns.

In terms of model structure, there are also limitations imposed by the pypomp library. We initially intended to include a broader set of covariates and use LASSO regularization for participant-specific feature selection to improve flexibility in modeling long-term dependencies. However, this was not feasible within the current framework.

There are also more robust approaches we could have used to evaluate model accuracy. In our analysis, we only applied masking to 20% of non-missing hourly observations per participant. Future work could explore a range of masking proportions to assess sensitivity to missingness. Additionally, masking could be applied in different structures, such as contiguous hourly blocks, full-day removals, or random missingness patterns.

Finally, there are limitations in our choice of benchmark and evaluation metric. We used masked subtotal error, defined as the difference between predicted and observed values on masked non-missing hourly observations. This choice was necessary because observed step counts during missing periods are recorded as zero, which may not reflect true step counts if participants were not wearing their devices. As a result, computing error against these zeros would be misleading. Instead, we restricted evaluation to masked data that was originally observed. If more time were available, we would conduct simulation studies with known latent step counts to compute a more standard metric such as RMSE.

# Acknowledgments {.unnumbered}
This report used the template available in the STATS 531 github. AI was used with Chatgpt 5.3Codex, extensively for coding, analysis, and formatting in Quarto, using the Codex and Quarto VScode extensions. Microsoft Copilot was also used for code autocomplete.

# Bibliography {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supplementary material {#sec-supp}

## Data Sources and Transformations

This project uses 2024 Intern Health Study (IHS) data provided by group member [insert Drew's group member number], who is currently conducting research on this topic.

The following data files were used for this project:

* FitbitHourly.csv
    + PARTICIPANTIDENTIFIER: unique ID for each doctor that participated in the study
    + STUDY_USER_ID: study-specific unique ID for each participant
    + time_utc: local time
      + date_utc: local date
      + hour_of_day_utc: local hour (0-23)
    + For Apple, Fitbit, and Garmin, the following data was recorded.
      + Steps: observed (recorded) step count during that hour (blank if no data was collected)
      + StepsMissing: binary indicator of whether data was collected for step count during that hour (1 = no data, 0 = data collected)
      + HRAvg: average heart rate during that hour
      + HRMin: minimum heart rate during that hour
      + HRMax: maximum heart rate during that hour
      + HRMissing: binary indicator of whether data was collected for heart rate during that hour (1 = no data, 0 = data collected)
      + HRVAvg: average heart rate variability during that hour
      + HRVMissing: binary indicator of whether data was collected for heart rate variability during that hour (1 = no data, 0 = data collected)
      + SleepMinutes: observed (recorded) minutes of sleep during that hour (blank if no data was collected)
      + SleepMissing: binary indicator of whether data was collected for sleep during that hour (1 = no data, 0 = data collected)
    + moodScore: scores range from 0 to 10 based on a survey
    + messageSent: binary indicator of whether the agent sent the participant a message during that hour (1 = message was sent, 0 = no message was sent)
    + messageStrategy: NOT SURE

## Masked Subtotal Benchmarking Metric

Using hourly observations with nonzero Fitbit step counts, we compute error at the daily level to align with the research goal of predicting 24-hour rewards. First, define

$$
\mathcal{H}_d = \{ t \in d : \text{hour } t \text{ was artificially masked and } y_t \text{ is observed} \}.
$$

Let $m_d = |\mathcal{H}_d|$ represent the 20\% of available data that was masked, but originally had nonzero step counts.

Next, define the true observed subtotal for this 20\% as

$$
S_d = \sum_{t \in \mathcal{H}_d} y_t,
$$

and the corresponding predicted subtotal as

$$
\hat{S}_d = \sum_{t \in \mathcal{H}_d} \hat{y}_t.
$$

The subtotal error is defined as

$$
E_d = \hat{S}_d - S_d = \sum_{t \in \mathcal{H}_d} (\hat{y}_t - y_t).
$$

It is important to normalize the subtotal error, as the analysis above shows that error increases with missingness. The normalized subtotal error is

$$
Z_d = \frac{E_d}{\sqrt{m_d}}.
$$

Finally, the benchmark is defined as

$$
\mathrm{RMSE}_{\text{subtotal}/\sqrt{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{D} \sum_{d=1}^{D} Z_d^2},
$$

# Local search?

## Additional background information
